title: How Are ETF Expense Ratios Paid? Everything Investors Nee...
description: Think your ETF is free? Discover how expense ratios are actually deducted from your returns, the true cost of fees over time, and what a 'good' ratio looks like.

mainEntity.name: How are expense ratios paid on ETFs?

acceptedAnswer.text: Expense ratios are not billed directly to you. Instead, the fund management company deducts a tiny fraction of the annual fee from the ETF's net asset value (NAV) every single day. The price you see quoted for the ETF already reflects this deduction.

mainEntity.name: What exactly is an ETF expense ratio?

acceptedAnswer.text: An ETF expense ratio is the annual fee charged by the fund managers to cover operating costs, marketing, administration, and management. It is expressed as a percentage of your total investment. For example, a 0.10% expense ratio means you pay $10 annually for every $10,000 invested.

mainEntity.name: How do ETF fees work over time?

acceptedAnswer.text: ETF fees continuously drag down your returns. Over decades, even a seemingly small difference in fees (like 0.75% vs 0.03%) can cost you tens of thousands of dollars in lost compounding growth. The fee reduces the capital you have available to earn returns in subsequent years.

mainEntity.name: Do I get a bill for my ETF expense ratio?

acceptedAnswer.text: No, you will never receive a bill for an expense ratio, nor will you see it itemized on your brokerage statement as a separate charge. It is automatically accrued daily out of the fund's assets.

mainEntity.name: What is a good expense ratio for an ETF?

acceptedAnswer.text: For broad market index funds (like S&P 500 ETFs), a good expense ratio is typically between 0.03% and 0.10%. For actively managed ETFs or highly specialized sector funds, expense ratios usually range from 0.50% to 1.50%. Anything over 1.00% should be heavily scrutinized.
